html-basics.title: Elements & Attributes
html-basics.description: Anatomy of HTML elements — tags, content, closing tags, and how attributes configure them

steps.heading: HTML is made of elements
steps.text: Every visible thing on a webpage is an HTML element — a heading, a paragraph, a link, an image. Elements tell the browser what the content means, not just how it looks. Choosing the right element is the most important HTML decision you make because it affects accessibility, SEO, and default browser behavior.

steps.heading: Opening tag
steps.text:
The opening tag names the element. `

` means paragraph. `

` means main heading. `` means anchor (link). The name determines how the browser renders the content and maps to an implicit ARIA role that screen readers use to identify what the element is.

steps.heading: Content
steps.text: Content lives between the tags. It can be text, other elements, or a mix of both. Nesting elements inside each other builds the tree structure of the page. Invalid nesting — like a block element inside an inline — triggers silent browser error correction that can break layout in unexpected ways.

steps.heading: Closing tag
steps.text:
The closing tag has a slash before the name. It ends the element. Some elements — like ``, `
`, `
`, `` — are void elements with no content and no closing tag.

steps.heading: Attributes
steps.text: Attributes go inside the opening tag and provide extra information. They come in name="value" pairs. Common ones: href (link URL), src (image source), alt (alt text), id (unique name), class (styling group).

steps.heading: Global attributes
steps.text: Several attributes work on any HTML element. `id` gives a unique name (used for CSS, JS, and anchor links). `class` groups elements for shared styling. `style` adds inline CSS (avoid for reusable styles). `data-*` stores custom data. `lang` sets the language. `title` adds a tooltip.

steps.heading: Boolean attributes
steps.text: Some attributes are boolean — their presence means true, their absence means false. You never write `disabled='true'` or `required='false'` — just `disabled` or no attribute at all. Common booleans: `disabled`, `required`, `checked`, `readonly`, `multiple`, `autoplay`.

syntax.label: id
syntax.note: Unique per page — used for JS & anchor links

syntax.label: src + alt
syntax.note: alt is required for accessibility & SEO

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use the most specific semantic element for each job. Choose <button> over <div onclick>, <a> over <span onclick>, <strong> over <b> for importance.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the closing tag — most elements need </tagname> or rendering breaks
Missing alt= on images — required for accessibility and fails HTML validation
Duplicating an id — id must be unique per page or querySelector returns wrong element

html-text.title: Text & Headings
html-text.description: Structure text with heading levels, paragraphs, and semantic inline formatting

steps.heading: Heading hierarchy
steps.text: HTML has six heading levels: <h1> through <h6>. <h1> is the most important — used once per page for the main title. Subsequent headings create a document outline that browsers, search engines, and screen readers all use.

steps.heading: Paragraphs
steps.text:
The

tag defines a paragraph. Browsers automatically add space above and below each paragraph. Never use
tags just to add vertical spacing — use CSS margins instead.

steps.heading: Semantic vs presentational
steps.text: <strong> and <em> carry meaning: importance and emphasis. Screen readers announce them differently. <b> and <i> are purely visual with no semantic weight. Prefer <strong>/<em> when the meaning matters.

steps.heading: Inline text elements
steps.text: HTML has many inline elements for rich text. <mark> highlights. <del> shows deleted text. <ins> shows inserted text. <small> reduces size. <sub> and <sup> create subscript and superscript.

steps.heading: Block quotes and code
steps.text: <blockquote> wraps content quoted from another source. <code> displays inline code in monospace. <pre> preserves whitespace and line breaks — combined with <code> for multi-line snippets.

steps.heading: Semantic annotation elements
steps.text: `<abbr>` marks abbreviations — the `title` attribute provides the expansion on hover. `<time>` marks dates and times with a machine-readable `datetime` attribute, helping search engines understand temporal content. `<cite>` wraps the title of a referenced creative work.

steps.heading: HTML entities
steps.text: Some characters cannot be typed in HTML directly because they would be interpreted as markup. HTML entities encode them: `<` for `<`, `>` for `>`, `&` for `&`, ` ` for a non-breaking space, `©` for ©, `—` for —.

syntax.label: Headings
syntax.note: h1 once per page; never skip levels

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use headings to create a logical document outline, not to control font size (use CSS for that). Always prefer semantic elements: <strong> over <b>, <em> over <i>.
commonMistakes: Using <h1> multiple times — one per page for SEO and accessibility
Skipping heading levels (h1 → h3) — headings must be sequential

Using
for paragraph spacing — use

tags and CSS margin instead

html-links-images.title: Links & Images
html-links-images.description: Navigate with anchor links and embed images with meaningful alt text

steps.heading: The anchor element
steps.text: The `<a>` (anchor) tag creates a link. The `href` attribute holds the destination — a URL, a path, an email address, or a `#fragment` for same-page jumps. The element has an implicit `role="link"` and is keyboard-navigable by default. Using `<div onclick>` as a fake link breaks keyboard access and requires ARIA remediation.

steps.heading: Absolute vs relative URLs
steps.text: Absolute URLs start with https:// — they work from anywhere. Relative URLs describe a path from the current page. Use relative links for internal navigation so the site works in any environment.

steps.heading: target and download
steps.text: target="_blank" opens the link in a new tab. Always add rel="noopener" with it for security. The download attribute tells the browser to download the file instead of navigating to it.

steps.heading: The element
steps.text: is a void element — no closing tag. src points to the image file. alt provides text for screen readers and when the image fails to load. width and height prevent layout shift.

steps.heading: Why alt text matters
steps.text: Alt text is read aloud by screen readers for blind users. It also appears when the image fails to load. Describe what is in the image, not just its file name. For decorative images, use an empty alt="" so screen readers skip it.

steps.heading: srcset — responsive images
steps.text: The `srcset` attribute provides multiple image versions at different resolutions. The browser picks the most appropriate one based on screen density and viewport size. Use `sizes` to tell the browser how wide the image will be displayed at each breakpoint.

steps.heading: loading="lazy"
steps.text: The `loading='lazy'` attribute defers loading of images that are below the viewport. They only load when the user scrolls near them — reducing initial page load time and bandwidth. Supported in all modern browsers. Use on below-the-fold images; keep `loading='eager'` (default) for hero images.

syntax.label: <a href>
syntax.note: href is required for a real link

syntax.label: Linked image
syntax.note: Wrap img in a to make it clickable

syntax.label: HTML comment
syntax.note: Invisible to users, visible in source

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use for all navigation. Use alt="" (empty) for purely decorative images. Set width/height on every to prevent content layout shift (CLS).
commonMistakes: Missing rel="noopener" on target="_blank" links — security vulnerability
alt="image.jpg" or alt="image" — describe the content, not the filename
Omitting width/height on images — causes layout shift as page loads

html-lists.title: Lists & Structure
html-lists.description: Organize content with ordered and unordered lists, and understand block vs inline layout

steps.heading: Unordered lists
steps.text: `<ul>` creates a bulleted list. Each item is wrapped in an `<li>` element. Use unordered lists when order doesn't matter — navigation menus, feature lists, tag sets. Screen readers announce the item count, so users know how many links or items to expect before diving in.

steps.heading: Ordered lists
steps.text: <ol> creates a numbered list. Items are still <li>. Use ordered lists when sequence matters — steps in a tutorial, rankings, a recipe. The browser handles the numbering automatically.

steps.heading: Nested lists
steps.text: Lists can be nested inside list items. An `<li>` can contain another `<ul>` or `<ol>`, creating hierarchical structures like a table of contents. The nested list must go inside the `<li>`, not after it — placing it outside creates invalid HTML that browsers silently repair in unpredictable ways.

steps.heading: Block vs inline elements
steps.text:
Block elements (
,

,

,
    ) start on a new line and take full width. Inline elements (, , , ) flow within text. This determines how elements stack and wrap.

steps.heading: <div> and <span>
steps.text: <div> is a generic block container — no semantic meaning, just grouping. <span> is a generic inline container. Use them for styling hooks or JavaScript targets when no semantic element fits. Prefer semantic elements when possible.

steps.heading: Description lists
steps.text: `<dl>` (description list) pairs terms with their definitions. `<dt>` is the term, `<dd>` is the description. Use it for glossaries, metadata panels, and FAQ pages — anywhere you have a term and its explanation.

steps.heading: Custom list markers
steps.text: CSS `list-style-type` changes bullet or number style: `disc`, `circle`, `decimal`, `lower-alpha`, and more. `list-style: none` removes markers entirely — used in navigation menus. `list-style-image` uses a custom image as the bullet.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Navigation menus should always be <ul> lists. Steps and procedures use <ol>. Use <div> and <span> only when no semantic element fits — they carry zero meaning.
commonMistakes:
Putting block elements (
,

) inside inline elements (, ) — invalid HTML

Using
to separate items — use a proper list instead
Skipping <li> inside <ul>/<ol> — direct children of lists must be <li>

html-dom.title: The DOM Tree
html-dom.description: How browsers parse HTML into a living tree of nodes

steps.heading: HTML is just text
steps.text: When your browser receives an HTML file, it sees plain text — tags, attributes, content. The DOM is the live, in-memory tree of JavaScript objects the browser builds from that text. The DOM is not HTML: malformed markup is silently corrected, and JavaScript modifications to the DOM never change the original HTML file.

steps.heading: Parsing starts at the top
steps.text: The browser reads your HTML top to bottom, creating a node for each element and connecting it to the tree. The `<html>` tag becomes the root. Scripts without `defer` or `async` block parsing — the browser stops building the DOM until the script downloads and executes, which is why scripts belong at the end of `<body>` or use `defer`.

steps.heading: Children branch outward
steps.text: Nested elements become child nodes of the element that wraps them — this creates the tree structure. The tree grows depth-first as the parser reads each opening tag, creates a node, and connects it to the current parent. Proper nesting is essential: an unclosed tag disrupts the parent-child chain and the browser silently repairs it, sometimes in unexpected ways.

steps.heading: Text is a node too
steps.text:
Every piece of text content between tags is its own text node in the DOM tree — a distinct node type separate from element nodes. This means `

Hello

` has two nodes: a `

` element node and a child text node containing "Hello". Whitespace between elements also becomes text nodes, which is why `element.children` (element nodes only) and `element.childNodes` (all nodes including text) return different results.

steps.heading: Selecting elements
steps.text: `querySelector()` returns the first element matching a CSS selector. `querySelectorAll()` returns all matches as a NodeList. `getElementById()` is faster but limited to ID selectors. Prefer `querySelector` for consistency with CSS syntax.

steps.heading: Reading and modifying content
steps.text: `textContent` safely reads and writes plain text — no HTML parsing, no security risk. Always prefer `textContent` for user-generated content. `innerHTML` parses HTML but opens XSS vulnerabilities if set with untrusted content. `setAttribute()` adds or updates any attribute.

steps.heading: Creating and inserting elements
steps.text: `document.createElement()` creates a new element in memory. Set its properties, then insert it with `appendChild()` or `insertBefore()`. `remove()` deletes an element. `cloneNode(true)` makes a deep copy including all children.

steps.heading: Events
steps.text: Events let you react to user actions. `addEventListener('click', handler)` attaches a handler to an element. The `event` object contains details like `event.target` (the clicked element). `removeEventListener` cancels the subscription.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use DOM APIs when dynamically changing the page without a framework. In React or Vue apps, prefer state + JSX to drive UI instead.
commonMistakes: Setting innerHTML with user input — XSS risk; use textContent for plain text instead
Calling querySelector before the DOM is loaded — wrap in DOMContentLoaded or put script at end of body
Forgetting querySelectorAll returns a NodeList not an Array — use Array.from() to access array methods like .map()

html-semantic.title: Semantic HTML
html-semantic.description: Meaningful elements that describe structure, not just appearance

steps.heading: The div soup problem
steps.text: Using only `<div>` elements tells browsers and screen readers nothing about your page's structure — every box looks the same. Semantic elements like `<header>`, `<main>`, and `<nav>` communicate purpose to assistive technology and search engines. They also create accessible landmarks that screen reader users can jump between directly.

steps.heading: <header>
steps.text: The `<header>` element contains introductory content — logo, site name, top navigation. There can be one per page or one per `<article>` or `<section>`. The top-level `<header>` maps to the ARIA `banner` landmark that screen reader users can jump to directly.

steps.heading: <nav>
steps.text: The `<nav>` element marks a block of navigation links — a landmark that screen readers expose so users can jump directly to navigation. A page can have multiple `<nav>` elements; use `aria-label` to distinguish them. Not every group of links warrants `<nav>` — reserve it for major site navigation.

steps.heading: <main> and <article>
steps.text: `<main>` contains the primary unique content of the page — there must be only one per page. `<article>` is for self-contained content like a blog post or news story that could be independently distributed. `<section>` groups thematically related content but always needs its surrounding context to be meaningful.

steps.heading: The full picture
steps.text: With semantic HTML, browsers, search engines, and assistive technologies all understand your page structure — no CSS classes required for meaning. ARIA landmark roles map directly to HTML5 elements, giving screen reader users one-keystroke navigation. Search engines use the heading hierarchy and `<article>` boundaries to determine content relevance.

steps.heading: section, aside, and figure
steps.text: `<section>` groups related content with its own heading — a chapter within a main area. `<aside>` holds tangentially related content: sidebars, pull quotes, related links. `<figure>` and `<figcaption>` pair an image with its caption.

steps.heading: time, address, and details/summary
steps.text: `<time>` marks a date/time with a machine-readable `datetime` attribute. `<address>` provides contact info for the nearest `<article>` or the whole document. `<details>` and `<summary>` create a native expandable widget without JavaScript.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Always prefer semantic elements over generic div. They improve accessibility (screen readers), SEO ranking, and code readability at zero extra cost.
commonMistakes: Using div for everything — semantic elements carry meaning for browsers and assistive tech
Multiple main elements on one page — only one is allowed
Skipping heading levels (h1 → h3) — headings must be sequential for accessibility

html-forms.title: Forms & Inputs
html-forms.description: Collect user input with forms, labels, and interactive controls

steps.heading: The <form> element
steps.text: A `<form>` wraps all input controls and submits data to a server. The `action` attribute sets the URL; `method` sets `GET` (data visible in URL) or `POST` (data in request body). Inputs without a `name` attribute are excluded from submission, which is a common source of missing data bugs.

steps.heading: <label> and <input>
steps.text: Every input should have a label. Connecting them with `for`/`id` makes the label clickable — expanding the tap target — and tells screen readers what the input is for. `placeholder` is not a substitute for a label: it disappears on focus and has poor contrast in most browsers.

steps.heading: <select> and <option>
steps.text: `<select>` creates a dropdown list where users pick from predefined choices. Each `<option>` provides a display label (shown to the user) and a `value` attribute (submitted to the server) — these can differ, e.g. `value="en"` displayed as "English". Use `<optgroup>` to visually group related options and the `multiple` attribute to allow multi-selection.

steps.heading: Checkbox and radio
steps.text: Checkboxes are independent — each can be checked or unchecked regardless of the others, making them ideal for multi-select scenarios like "pick all that apply". Radio buttons that share the same `name` attribute form a group where only one can be active at a time, enforced automatically by the browser. The `checked` attribute sets the initial state, and the `:checked` CSS pseudo-class lets you style the selected state without JavaScript.

steps.heading: <button type="submit">
steps.text: The submit button sends the form. `type="submit"` is the default for all `<button>` elements inside a form — a button without an explicit type will accidentally trigger submission. `type="button"` prevents submission and is safe to use for JavaScript-driven actions. Disable the button during processing to prevent double-submits.

steps.heading: HTML5 built-in validation
steps.text: Add required, minlength, maxlength, pattern, min, max directly in HTML — the browser validates before submit, no JavaScript needed. required alone blocks empty submission.

steps.heading: Constraint validation API
steps.text: For custom error messages or real-time feedback, use the Constraint Validation API. setCustomValidity() overrides the browser message. checkValidity() returns true/false without submitting.

steps.heading: fieldset and legend
steps.text: `<fieldset>` groups related inputs. `<legend>` labels the group. Screen readers read the legend before each input in the group, providing context. This is especially important for radio button groups and checkbox groups where each input makes no sense without the group label.

steps.heading: Specialized input types
steps.text: HTML5 added many `<input>` types beyond text: `date` shows a date picker, `color` shows a color wheel, `range` renders a slider, `number` limits to numeric input, `email` validates email format. Mobile keyboards adapt to the type automatically.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use native form elements for all user input — they have built-in accessibility, keyboard support, and validation for free. Use FormData to read values on submit.
commonMistakes: Missing label for every input — always pair label with for/id so screen readers work
Forgetting e.preventDefault() when handling submit in JS — causes page reload
Not setting name= on inputs — FormData and form submission will not capture the value
Relying only on client-side validation — always re-validate on the server
Using pattern without title= — browser shows generic "Please match format" without a hint

html-accessibility.title: Accessibility (a11y)
html-accessibility.description: ARIA roles, keyboard navigation, focus management — build for all users from the start

steps.heading: Who needs accessibility?
steps.text: About 15% of people have a disability. Screen reader users navigate by headings and landmarks. Keyboard users can't use a mouse. Color-blind users need contrast. Semantic HTML gives you most of this for free — ARIA fills the gaps.

steps.heading: aria-label and aria-labelledby
steps.text: When visible text isn't enough, `aria-label` provides an accessible name directly. Icons-only buttons, close buttons, and image-only inputs all need descriptive labels for screen readers. Without a label, assistive technology reads the element type only — "button" — which tells the user nothing. Use `aria-labelledby` to point to an existing visible element instead of duplicating text.

steps.heading: Focus management
steps.text: Keyboard users Tab through interactive elements. Focus must be visible — never remove :focus styles without replacing them. Use :focus-visible to show rings only for keyboard navigation, not clicks.

steps.heading: tabindex
steps.text: tabindex="0" makes any element keyboard-focusable in natural DOM order. tabindex="-1" allows programmatic focus (el.focus()) but removes from tab sequence. Never use tabindex > 0 — it breaks natural order.

steps.heading: ARIA roles and live regions
steps.text: role= overrides the element's implicit role. ARIA live regions announce dynamic content changes to screen readers. aria-live='polite' waits for silence, 'assertive' interrupts immediately.

steps.heading: aria-expanded and aria-controls
steps.text: When a button toggles a panel (accordion, dropdown, modal), ARIA attributes communicate the open/closed state. `aria-expanded='false'` tells screen readers the panel is collapsed. `aria-controls` points to the element being controlled by ID. Update these with JavaScript as the state changes.

steps.heading: Color contrast and visual accessibility
steps.text: Color contrast is the ratio between text color and background color. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text. Low contrast affects colorblind users, users on bright screens, and older users. Never convey information with color alone — always add a shape, icon, or text label.

syntax.label: aria-label
syntax.note: Overrides button text for screen readers

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use semantic HTML first — <button>, <a>, <input>, headings, landmarks. Add ARIA only when HTML semantics aren't enough. 'No ARIA is better than bad ARIA.'
commonMistakes: Removing :focus outline without replacement — keyboard users lose all navigation feedback
Using aria-label on non-interactive elements — doesn't help unless paired with role=
role="button" without onkeydown handler — click works with mouse but not Enter/Space
aria-hidden="true" on focusable elements — screen reader skips it but keyboard still reaches it

html-media.title: Media & Embeds
html-media.description: Embed video, audio, iframes, and responsive images with native HTML elements

steps.heading: <iframe> — page within a page
steps.text: An <iframe> embeds another HTML document inside your page. YouTube embeds, Google Maps, and CodePen previews all use iframes. The embedded content runs in its own isolated browsing context.

steps.heading: <video>
steps.text: The <video> element embeds video natively. The controls attribute adds play/pause/volume UI. Include multiple <source> elements for browser compatibility — the browser picks the first format it supports.

steps.heading: <audio>
steps.text: `<audio>` works the same as `<video>` but for sound. The `controls` attribute adds a native player. Without `controls`, the audio is invisible and plays only via JavaScript — useful for background music. Provide multiple formats (`mp3` and `ogg`) for broadest browser compatibility.

steps.heading: <picture> — responsive images
steps.text: serves different images based on screen size or format support. The browser picks the first matching . The fallback is always required — it's what actually renders the image.

steps.heading: <code> and <pre>
steps.text: <code> displays inline code in monospace font. Wrap it in <pre> to preserve whitespace and newlines for multi-line snippets. This is how documentation sites, READMEs, and tech blogs show code examples.

steps.heading: loading="lazy" for images and iframes
steps.text: The `loading='lazy'` attribute defers images and iframes until they near the viewport. The browser only fetches them when the user scrolls close — reducing initial page load. Works natively without JavaScript.

steps.heading: Video autoplay rules
steps.text: Browsers block autoplay video with sound by default. To autoplay, the video must be `muted`. Add `loop` for background videos that repeat silently. Use `playsinline` on iOS to prevent full-screen takeover.

syntax.label: Video autoplay
syntax.note: muted required for autoplay in browsers

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use <video>/<audio> for self-hosted media. Use <iframe> for third-party embeds (YouTube, Maps). Use <picture> when you need different images for different screen sizes or format support.
commonMistakes: Missing title on <iframe> — required for screen readers
Autoplay video without muted — browsers block autoplay with sound by default
Omitting the fallback in — the img is required, source elements are optional
